title: Exchange-frustrated quadrupoles on the honeycomb lattice: Flavor-wave spectra, classical degeneracies and parton constructions
authors: Partha Sarker, Han Ma, Urban F. P. Seifert
abstract: We study the quadrupolar Kitaev model, an $S=1$ honeycomb-lattice model with frustrated bond-dependent quadrupolar interactions. Using complementary methods and expanding around controlled limits, we uncover several intertwined structures. First, a semiclassical variational analysis based on $\mathrm{SU}(3)$ flavor theory reveals an extensively degenerate manifold of classical mean-field ground states, suggesting that quantum fluctuations may stabilize a quantum-disordered phase. Second, in the bond-anisotropic limit, perturbation theory is used to derive effective low-energy Hamiltonians, which crucially depend on the presence (or absence) of a residual symmetry $\mathcal{M}$ of combined lattice reflection and discrete spin rotation. A Majorana parton construction uncovers an exact $\mathbb Z_2$ gauge structure and motivates possible confined and deconfined phases driven by gauge-charge condensation, consistent with the effective theories obtained in anisotropic limit. Further, within the same parton formalism, different Majorana mean-field ansätze produce both gapless and gapped candidate quantum-disordered states, distinguished by linear versus projective implementations of $\mathcal M$. Our results highlight frustrated quadrupolar interactions as a route to quantum-disordered phases, relevant to $S \geq 1$ Kitaev materials and Rydberg-array quantum simulators.
summary: This paper explores a spin-1 honeycomb model with frustrated quadrupolar interactions. It demonstrates that the system possesses an extensive classical degeneracy and an emergent Z2 gauge structure, suggesting the existence of quantum-disordered phases. These findings are particularly relevant for understanding S >= 1 Kitaev materials and Rydberg-array simulators.
main_problem: Investigating whether exchange-frustrated quadrupolar interactions on a honeycomb lattice can stabilize quantum-disordered phases, such as quantum spin liquids, instead of long-range multipolar order.
main_result: The study identifies an extensive manifold of classical ground states and an emergent Z2 gauge structure, finding that quantum fluctuations do not select a specific symmetry-broken state via order-by-disorder, pointing toward possible gapped or gapless quantum-disordered phases.
method: The authors use a combination of SU(3) flavor-wave theory, Majorana parton construction, real-space and degenerate perturbation theory, and self-consistent Majorana mean-field theory.
model_or_system: A spin-1 quadrupolar Kitaev model on a honeycomb lattice featuring bond-dependent, frustrated rank-2 symmetric tensor interactions.
key_observables: Flavor-wave energy bands, quadrupolar expectation values, Z2 gauge structure, loop operators (W_L), and Majorana fermion dispersions.
important_parameters: Exchange couplings (Jx, Jy, Jz), Zeeman magnetic field (h), single-ion anisotropy (D), and the bond-anisotropic limit.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on controlled limits (large-D, large-h, or bond-anisotropy) and mean-field approximations for the parton construction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates frustrated interactions and plaquette operators; Figure 4 shows the four-color state configurations and flux lines; Figure 8 displays the calculated Majorana fermion energy gap and momentum space contours.
paper_structure: The paper begins by introducing the quadrupolar Kitaev model, followed by a semiclassical analysis of classical degeneracies, an investigation of instabilities using flavor-wave theory, a perturbative study of the anisotropic limit, and finally a Majorana parton construction to identify candidate quantum-disordered phases.
why_interesting: The study's relevance to Rydberg-array quantum simulators and the exploration of emergent gauge structures and fractionalized excitations are highly relevant to many-body dynamics and synthetic quantum matter.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Illustration of frustrated bond-dependent interac- tions on the honeycomb lattice as described by the Hamil- tonian in Eq. (1). For example, the antiferroquadrupolar interaction on the y-bond may favor a spin-nematic state with a director along the (1, 0, 1) axis, competing with an antiferroquadrupolar interaction on the x-bond which can be maximized by a director along the (0, 1, 1) direction. The red and green tori indicate the probability distribution for a measurement to observe the spin in the respective direc- tion, i.e. | ⟨S(θ, φ)|d⟩|2 with |S(θ, φ)⟩being a spin-coherent state. (b) Illustration of hexagonal plaquette operator WL = Q

FIG. 2. The band structure of flavor-wave excitations on top of the spin-polarized reference state evolves as the magnetic field lowered. At h ≈0.91J, the flavor wave excitations be- come gapless and obtain a macroscopic occupation at all mo- menta, i.e. ⟨bk⟩= constant. As a result, the polarized ref- erence state for a Zeeman field along the [111] axis becomes unstable.

FIG. 3. We analyze the scaling of the bandgap and low- est band bandwidth for trivial reference states stabilized by (a) [111] Zeeman field, and (b) [111] easy-plane single- ion anisotropy (SIA). The bandgap scales linearly for the magnetic-field deformation, while it displays an (approxi- mate) square-root behaviour for the SIA. The bandwidth of the lowest band for the reference state stabilized by the SIA deformation vanishes, reflecting its flatness in the entire large- D/J phase [see panel (b)], while it exhibits an (approximate) square-root scaling in the field-polarized regime as shown in panel (a).

FIG. 4. (a) Four color states with associated flux lines to them. (b) The extensive nature of mean field ground states can always be mapped to an emergent gauge theory with charges placed on the lattice and flux lines on the bonds. (c) One representative mean field ground state configuration, (d) a second mean field ground state which differs from (c) at only one hexagon.
